abstract.t: Use of TLS 1.3, which fixes some known deficiencies in TLS 1.2, is growing. This document specifies that no changes will be approved for TLS 1.2 outside of urgent security fixes (as determined by TLS Working Group consensus), new TLS Exporter Labels, and new Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation (ALPN) Protocol IDs. This applies to TLS only; it does not apply to DTLS (in any DTLS version).

section.name: Introduction
section.t: TLS 1.3 fixes most known deficiencies with TLS 1.2 and its use is growing. Some examples of the fixes include encrypting more of the traffic so that it is not readable by outsiders and removing most cryptographic primitives that are now considered weak. Importantly, TLS 1.3 enjoys robust security proofs.

Both versions have several extension points. Items like new cryptographic algorithms, new supported groups (formerly "named curves"), etc., can be added without defining a new protocol. This document specifies that no changes will be approved for TLS 1.2 outside of urgent security fixes (as determined by TLS Working Group consensus) and the exceptions listed in .
This applies to TLS only. As such, it does not apply to DTLS, in any DTLS version.

section.name: Implications for Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)
section.t: Cryptographically relevant quantum computers, once available, are likely to greatly lessen the time and effort needed to break RSA, finite-field-based Diffie-Hellman (FFDH), or Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) which are currently used in TLS. In 2016, the US National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) started a multi-year effort to standardize algorithms that will be "safe" once quantum computers are feasible . Initial discussions in the IETF community happened around the same time .
In 2024, NIST released standards for , , and . Many other countries and organizations are publishing their roadmaps, including the multi-national standards organization ETSI .

While the industry was waiting for NIST to finish standardization, the IETF has had several efforts underway. A working group was formed in early 2023 to work on the use of Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) in IETF protocols . Several other working groups, including TLS , are working on specifications to support hybrid algorithms and identifiers, for use during a transition from classic to a post-quantum world.

It is important to note that effort within the TLS Working Group is focused exclusively on TLS 1.3 or later. Put bluntly, PQC for TLS 1.2 will not be specified (see ) at any time; anyone wishing to deploy PQC should expect to use TLS 1.3.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: This entire document is about security and provides post-quantum security concerns as an additional reason to upgrade to TLS 1.3.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: No TLS registries are being closed by this document. Rather, this document modifies the instructions to IANA and the TLS Designated Experts to constrain the type of entries that can be added to existing registries.
This document does not introduce any new limitations on the registrations for either of the following two registries:

li.t: TLS Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation (ALPN) Protocol IDs

section.t: The following note has been added to the other TLS registries:

blockquote.t: Any TLS entry added after the IESG approves publication of RFC 9851 is intended for TLS 1.3 or later, and makes no similar requirement on DTLS. Such entries should have an informal indication like "For TLS 1.3 or later" in that entry, such as the "Comment" column.

section.t: At the time of publication, the note has been added to the following TLS registries:
Any TLS registry created after this document is approved for publication should indicate whether the actions defined here are applicable.

author.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology
National Institute of Standards and Technology
National Institute of Standards and Technology

front.title: CYBER; Migration strategies and recommendations to Quantum Safe schemes

section.name: Acknowledgments
section.t: We gratefully acknowledge , , and of IANA for their help in revising and clarifying .
